1AVB.summary: First degree AV block is a conduction delay at the atrioventricular node characterized by a consistently prolonged PR interval exceeding 200ms. It is generally benign but may indicate underlying conduction disease or drug effect.
ecg_features: Every P wave is followed by a QRS complex
1AVB.clinical_significance: First degree AV block is usually a benign finding but can progress to higher-degree block, especially in the setting of acute inferior MI, myocarditis, or drug toxicity. It may be a marker of underlying structural heart disease, vagal tone elevation, or electrolyte disturbance. In asymptomatic patients, no treatment is required, but monitoring for progression is important.
1AVB.typical_patient: Older adults with structural heart disease, athletes with high vagal tone, or patients on AV-nodal blocking medications such as digoxin, beta-blockers, or calcium channel blockers.
management_hints: Review current medications (digoxin, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers)
Serial ECG monitoring for progression to higher-degree block
Cardiology referral if symptomatic or associated with bundle branch block
1AVB.report_template: Sinus rhythm with first degree AV block (PR interval prolonged). No evidence of higher-degree block. Clinical correlation recommended.

2AVB.summary: Second degree AV block is an intermittent failure of atrial impulse conduction to the ventricles, classified as Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) with progressive PR prolongation or Mobitz type II with fixed PR and sudden dropped beats.
ecg_features: Mobitz I: progressive PR prolongation before dropped beat
Mobitz II: fixed PR interval with sudden non-conducted P wave
Ventricular rate less than atrial rate
2AVB.clinical_significance: Mobitz type I is often benign and associated with inferior MI or increased vagal tone. Mobitz type II is more serious, indicating infranodal disease, and carries a risk of progression to complete heart block. Prompt cardiology evaluation is required especially for Mobitz type II.
2AVB.typical_patient: Elderly patients with structural heart disease or acute inferior myocardial infarction. Mobitz I may occur in athletes or younger patients with high vagal tone.
management_hints: Mobitz II requires urgent cardiology referral and consideration of pacemaker
Discontinue AV-nodal blocking drugs if implicated
Atropine for symptomatic bradycardia as bridge therapy
2AVB.report_template: Second degree AV block present. Cardiology review recommended to determine type and assess need for pacing.

3AVB.summary: Third degree (complete) AV block is complete dissociation between atrial and ventricular activity, with the ventricles driven by an escape rhythm. This is a critical arrhythmia requiring urgent intervention.
ecg_features: P waves and QRS complexes are independent
Wide QRS if ventricular escape; narrow if junctional escape
Atrial rate faster than ventricular rate
3AVB.clinical_significance: Complete heart block is a medical emergency associated with syncope, hemodynamic compromise, and risk of asystole. It may be caused by inferior MI (usually transient), anterior MI (usually permanent), infiltrative disease, or drug toxicity. Permanent pacemaker implantation is typically required.
3AVB.typical_patient: Elderly patients with structural heart disease or acute MI. Can occur in younger patients with congenital complete heart block or myocarditis.
management_hints: Permanent pacemaker implantation in most cases
3AVB.report_template: Complete third degree AV block with AV dissociation and ventricular escape rhythm. Urgent cardiology review and pacing evaluation required.

ABQRS.summary: Abnormal QRS morphology indicating disturbance in ventricular depolarization. This is a non-specific finding that encompasses a variety of intraventricular conduction abnormalities.
ecg_features: Widened or morphologically abnormal QRS complex
May include notching, slurring, or fragmentation
Not meeting criteria for complete bundle branch block
ABQRS.clinical_significance: Abnormal QRS morphology is a non-specific marker of ventricular conduction disturbance. It may reflect underlying ischemia, infarction, cardiomyopathy, or electrolyte abnormality. Further characterization of the underlying cause is essential.
ABQRS.typical_patient: Patients with known or suspected structural heart disease, ischemia, or electrolyte abnormalities.
management_hints: Correlate with clinical history and symptoms
Cardiology referral if new or unexplained
ABQRS.report_template: Abnormal QRS morphology noted. Clinical correlation and further evaluation recommended.

AFIB.summary: Atrial fibrillation is an irregularly irregular supraventricular arrhythmia characterized by chaotic atrial activity and absence of distinct P waves, resulting in an irregular ventricular response.
ecg_features: Narrow QRS unless aberrant conduction or pre-existing bundle branch block
AFIB.clinical_significance: Atrial fibrillation is the most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia and significantly increases the risk of stroke and heart failure. Rate control and anticoagulation are cornerstones of management. Rhythm control may be considered in symptomatic patients. CHA2DS2-VASc score should guide anticoagulation decisions.
AFIB.typical_patient: Elderly patients with hypertension, heart failure, valvular disease, or coronary artery disease. Increasingly common with advancing age.
management_hints: Rate control with beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers
Rhythm control with cardioversion or antiarrhythmics if indicated
Echocardiogram to assess for structural disease
AFIB.report_template: Atrial fibrillation with ventricular rate. Anticoagulation assessment and cardiology review recommended.

AFLT.summary: Atrial flutter is a rapid regular atrial arrhythmia typically at 250-350 bpm with characteristic sawtooth flutter waves, usually conducting to the ventricles with a fixed ratio (most commonly 2:1).
ecg_features: Sawtooth flutter waves (F waves) at 250-350 bpm
Ventricular rate typically 150 bpm with 2:1 block
AFLT.clinical_significance: Atrial flutter carries similar thromboembolic risk to atrial fibrillation and requires anticoagulation. It is often unstable and may convert to atrial fibrillation. Catheter ablation has high success rates for typical flutter. Rate control is often challenging.
AFLT.typical_patient: Patients with structural heart disease, chronic lung disease, or post-cardiac surgery. Similar demographics to atrial fibrillation.
management_hints: Rate control with AV-nodal blocking agents
Anticoagulation as per atrial fibrillation guidelines
Catheter ablation for recurrent typical flutter
AFLT.report_template: Atrial flutter with AV block and ventricular rate. Rate control and anticoagulation assessment required.

ALMI.summary: Anterolateral myocardial infarction indicates ischemic injury involving the anterior and lateral walls of the left ventricle, typically from occlusion of the left anterior descending or left circumflex artery.
ecg_features: Pathological Q waves in I, aVL, V2-V6
ST elevation or depression in anterolateral leads
T wave inversion in I, aVL, V4-V6
Loss of R wave progression in anterior chest leads
ALMI.clinical_significance: Anterolateral MI represents a large territory of myocardium at risk, often resulting in significant left ventricular dysfunction. Early reperfusion therapy is critical. Long-term risk of heart failure and ventricular arrhythmias is substantial.
ALMI.typical_patient: Middle-aged to elderly patients with risk factors for coronary artery disease including hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, and smoking.
management_hints: Urgent reperfusion if acute (primary PCI or thrombolysis)
ALMI.report_template: ECG findings consistent with anterolateral myocardial infarction. Urgent clinical correlation and cardiology review required.

AMI.summary: Anterior myocardial infarction involves ischemic injury to the anterior wall of the left ventricle, typically due to occlusion of the left anterior descending coronary artery.
ecg_features: Poor R wave progression in anterior leads
T wave inversion in anterior leads
AMI.clinical_significance: Anterior MI carries the highest mortality among MI territories due to involvement of a large myocardial mass. Anterior STEMI requires immediate cath lab activation. Risk of complete heart block, ventricular aneurysm, and cardiogenic shock is significant.
AMI.typical_patient: Patients with significant coronary artery disease, typically older males with multiple cardiovascular risk factors.
management_hints: Immediate cath lab activation for STEMI
Consideration of ICD if EF severely reduced
AMI.report_template: ECG changes consistent with anterior myocardial infarction. Urgent clinical and laboratory correlation required.

ANEUR.summary: ST-T changes compatible with left ventricular aneurysm, characterized by persistent ST elevation in leads overlying a previous transmural myocardial infarction territory.
ecg_features: Persistent ST elevation weeks to months after MI
Pathological Q waves in the same territory
ST elevation typically concave or convex upward
No reciprocal ST depression (unlike acute STEMI)
T waves may be upright or inverted
ANEUR.clinical_significance: Ventricular aneurysm is a late complication of transmural MI, associated with risk of heart failure, ventricular arrhythmias, and mural thrombus with systemic embolization. Echocardiogram is essential for confirmation.
ANEUR.typical_patient: Patients with history of large transmural anterior MI, often with delayed or no reperfusion therapy.
management_hints: Echocardiogram to confirm aneurysm and assess for thrombus
Surgical aneurysm resection in selected cases
ANEUR.report_template: Persistent ST elevation with Q waves in anterior leads, consistent with previous MI and possible ventricular aneurysm. Echocardiogram recommended.

ASMI.summary: Anteroseptal myocardial infarction involves ischemic injury to the anterior septum and anterior wall, typically due to proximal LAD occlusion.
ecg_features: Pathological Q waves or QS complexes in V1-V3
Poor R wave progression in V1-V4
ASMI.clinical_significance: Anteroseptal MI indicates involvement of the interventricular septum and anterior free wall. Complete LBBB may develop. Risk of septal rupture in acute setting. Long-term LV dysfunction depends on infarct size.
ASMI.typical_patient: Patients with coronary artery disease, particularly proximal LAD disease.
ASMI.report_template: ECG changes consistent with anteroseptal myocardial infarction. Clinical and laboratory correlation required.

BIGU.summary: Bigeminal pattern refers to a rhythm where every normal beat is followed by a premature beat (either atrial or ventricular), creating a repeating paired pattern.
ecg_features: Premature beats may be wide (ventricular) or narrow (atrial)
Effective heart rate may be halved
BIGU.clinical_significance: Bigeminy may be benign in structurally normal hearts but can indicate underlying ischemia, electrolyte disturbance, or drug toxicity, especially digitalis. Frequent ventricular bigeminy can cause hemodynamic compromise if sustained.
BIGU.typical_patient: Can occur in any age group; more concerning in patients with structural heart disease or on cardiac medications.
management_hints: Cardiology referral if symptomatic or high burden
BIGU.report_template: Bigeminal pattern noted. Clinical correlation, electrolyte check, and medication review recommended.

CLBBB.summary: Complete left bundle branch block is a major intraventricular conduction disturbance with QRS duration ≥120ms and characteristic morphology, most commonly due to ischemic heart disease.
ecg_features: Broad notched R waves in I, aVL, V5-V6
QS or rS pattern in V1-V2
No septal Q waves in lateral leads
CLBBB.clinical_significance: New LBBB in the setting of chest pain should be treated as STEMI equivalent. Chronic LBBB indicates significant conduction disease and is associated with cardiomyopathy, ischemic heart disease, and increased mortality. Standard ischemia assessment is limited in the presence of LBBB.
CLBBB.typical_patient: Older patients with structural heart disease, hypertension, or known coronary artery disease.
management_hints: New LBBB with chest pain: activate cath lab (Sgarbossa criteria)
Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) if EF ≤35% with LBBB
CLBBB.report_template: Complete left bundle branch block present. Clinical correlation required; new LBBB with symptoms warrants urgent evaluation.

CRBBB.summary: Complete right bundle branch block is characterized by delayed right ventricular activation with QRS ≥120ms and RSR' pattern in V1, commonly found in patients with or without structural heart disease.
ecg_features: RSR' or rSR' pattern in V1-V2 (M-shaped)
Wide slurred S waves in I, aVL, V5-V6
Right axis deviation may be present
CRBBB.clinical_significance: Isolated RBBB may be a normal variant in younger patients but warrants investigation for underlying cardiopulmonary disease when new. Combined with left anterior or posterior fascicular block (bifascicular block) increases risk of complete heart block.
CRBBB.typical_patient: Wide age range; may be incidental in otherwise healthy individuals or associated with cardiopulmonary disease.
management_hints: New RBBB: investigate for acute pulmonary embolism or MI
Bifascicular block: monitor for progression to complete heart block
Cardiology referral if symptomatic or new
CRBBB.report_template: Complete right bundle branch block. Clinical correlation recommended; new RBBB warrants investigation for underlying cause.

DIG.summary: ECG changes compatible with digitalis effect, characterized by sagging ST depression and T wave changes, typically in patients receiving digoxin therapy.
ecg_features: Sagging or scooping ST segment depression
Changes most prominent in lateral leads
DIG.clinical_significance: Digitalis effect is an expected ECG finding in patients on therapeutic digoxin and does not necessarily indicate toxicity. Digitalis toxicity causes a wide spectrum of arrhythmias including AV block, junctional rhythms, and ventricular arrhythmias. Serum digoxin level should be checked.
DIG.typical_patient: Patients with atrial fibrillation or heart failure receiving digoxin therapy, typically elderly.
management_hints: Assess for signs of toxicity (nausea, visual disturbances)
DIG.report_template: ST-T changes compatible with digitalis effect. Check digoxin level and electrolytes.

EL.summary: ECG changes compatible with electrolyte disturbance or drug effect, most commonly manifested as QT prolongation with associated T wave abnormalities.
EL.clinical_significance: Electrolyte abnormalities, particularly hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and hypocalcemia, can prolong the QT interval and predispose to potentially fatal arrhythmias including torsades de pointes. Multiple drugs also prolong the QT interval.
EL.typical_patient: Patients on diuretics, antiarrhythmics, antibiotics, or antipsychotics; patients with renal failure or malnutrition.
management_hints: Review all medications for QT-prolonging drugs
EL.report_template: QT prolongation and non-specific ST-T changes compatible with electrolyte disturbance or drug effect. Electrolyte panel and medication review required.

HVOLT.summary: High QRS voltage indicates increased amplitude of QRS complexes, often meeting voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy, particularly in chest leads.
ecg_features: S in V1 + R in V5 or V6 > 35mm (Sokolow-Lyon)
Increased QRS amplitude in precordial leads
HVOLT.clinical_significance: High voltage alone has low specificity for true LVH. It is common in young, thin, athletic individuals as a normal variant. In the appropriate clinical context (hypertension, aortic stenosis), it supports the diagnosis of LVH and warrants echocardiographic confirmation.
HVOLT.typical_patient: Young thin adults as a normal variant, or older patients with hypertension or valvular disease.
management_hints: Echocardiogram to assess true LV mass
Clinically correlate with patient age and body habitus
Cardiology referral if echocardiogram confirms LVH
HVOLT.report_template: High QRS voltage noted, meeting criteria suggestive of left ventricular hypertrophy. Echocardiogram recommended for confirmation.

ILBBB.summary: Incomplete left bundle branch block is a partial conduction delay in the left bundle system with QRS duration between 110-119ms and LBBB morphological features.
ecg_features: Broad or notched R waves in I, aVL, V5-V6
ILBBB.clinical_significance: Incomplete LBBB may represent early or partial left bundle disease. It carries less prognostic significance than complete LBBB but warrants clinical evaluation for underlying structural heart disease.
ILBBB.typical_patient: Similar to complete LBBB but often earlier stage of conduction disease.
management_hints: Monitor for progression to complete LBBB
ILBBB.report_template: Incomplete left bundle branch block pattern noted. Clinical correlation and echocardiogram recommended.

ILMI.summary: Inferolateral myocardial infarction involves combined inferior and lateral wall ischemic injury, typically from occlusion of a dominant RCA or LCx artery.
ecg_features: Pathological Q waves in II, III, aVF and V5-V6
ST changes in inferior and lateral leads
Reciprocal changes in aVL and I
ILMI.clinical_significance: Inferolateral MI represents a larger territory than isolated inferior MI and carries greater risk of LV dysfunction. Right ventricular involvement should be assessed with right-sided leads.
ILMI.typical_patient: Patients with multi-vessel coronary artery disease, often with prior cardiovascular history.
management_hints: Right-sided ECG leads to assess RV involvement
ILMI.report_template: ECG changes consistent with inferolateral myocardial infarction. Urgent clinical correlation required.

IMI.summary: Inferior myocardial infarction involves ischemic injury to the inferior wall of the left ventricle, typically from right coronary artery occlusion.
ecg_features: Pathological Q waves in II, III, aVF
ST elevation in II, III, aVF (if acute)
Reciprocal ST depression in I, aVL
T wave inversion in inferior leads
ST depression in V1-V2 suggests posterior extension
IMI.clinical_significance: Inferior MI is the most common MI territory. Right ventricular infarction occurs in up to 40% of inferior MIs and must be screened for with right-sided leads as it affects management. Complete heart block may complicate inferior MI.
IMI.typical_patient: Patients with coronary artery disease; inferior MI more common in diabetics and women who may present atypically.
management_hints: Right-sided leads (V3R-V5R) to exclude RV infarction
Avoid nitrates if RV infarction present
Primary PCI or thrombolysis if acute STEMI
IMI.report_template: ECG changes consistent with inferior myocardial infarction. Right-sided leads and urgent clinical correlation required.

INJAL.summary: Subendocardial injury pattern in anterolateral leads, manifested as ST depression and T wave inversion in I, aVL, and V4-V6, suggesting non-transmural ischemia or NSTEMI.
ecg_features: ST depression in I, aVL, V4-V6
INJAL.clinical_significance: Anterolateral subendocardial injury pattern is a high-risk ECG finding suggesting significant anterolateral ischemia or NSTEMI. Prompt evaluation with serial troponins and cardiology review is mandatory.
INJAL.typical_patient: Patients with known or suspected coronary artery disease presenting with chest pain or equivalent symptoms.
INJAL.report_template: ST depression and T wave changes in anterolateral leads consistent with subendocardial injury. Serial troponin and urgent cardiology review required.

INJAS.summary: Subendocardial injury in anteroseptal leads, with ST depression and T wave inversion in V1-V4, indicating anteroseptal ischemia or NSTEMI.
INJAS.clinical_significance: Deep T wave inversion in anteroseptal leads (Wellens pattern) may indicate critical proximal LAD stenosis requiring urgent intervention even in the absence of ongoing pain.
INJAS.typical_patient: Patients presenting with unstable angina or NSTEMI, especially with proximal LAD disease.
INJAS.report_template: Anteroseptal ST-T changes consistent with subendocardial injury. Serial troponin and urgent cardiology review required.

INJIL.summary: Subendocardial injury in inferolateral leads with ST depression and T wave inversion in inferior and lateral leads, consistent with inferolateral NSTEMI.
ecg_features: ST depression in II, III, aVF, V5-V6
INJIL.clinical_significance: Inferolateral subendocardial injury pattern indicates significant ischemia in the inferior and lateral territories, often from multi-vessel disease or dominant circumflex/RCA involvement.
INJIL.typical_patient: Patients with multi-vessel coronary artery disease presenting with acute coronary syndrome.
INJIL.report_template: Inferolateral ST-T changes consistent with subendocardial injury. Serial troponin and urgent cardiology review required.

INJIN.summary: Subendocardial injury in inferior leads with ST depression and T wave inversion in II, III, and aVF, suggesting inferior NSTEMI or significant inferior ischemia.
ecg_features: ST depression in II, III, aVF
INJIN.clinical_significance: Inferior subendocardial injury warrants urgent evaluation for NSTEMI. Serial troponins and continuous monitoring are required. Right-sided leads should be obtained.
INJIN.typical_patient: Patients with known or suspected RCA or LCx disease.
INJIN.report_template: Inferior ST-T changes consistent with subendocardial injury. Serial troponin and cardiology review required.

INJLA.summary: Subendocardial injury in lateral leads with ST depression and T wave inversion in I, aVL, V5-V6, suggesting lateral NSTEMI or significant lateral ischemia.
ecg_features: ST depression in I, aVL, V5-V6
INJLA.clinical_significance: Lateral subendocardial injury indicates significant ischemia in the lateral wall territory, often from left circumflex artery disease.
INJLA.typical_patient: Patients with circumflex or obtuse marginal artery disease.
INJLA.report_template: Lateral ST-T changes consistent with subendocardial injury. Serial troponin and cardiology review required.

INVT.summary: Inverted T waves are a non-specific finding that may reflect ischemia, ventricular strain, bundle branch block, or be a normal variant in certain leads.
ecg_features: Associated ST changes may be present
INVT.clinical_significance: T wave inversion can be a marker of myocardial ischemia, ventricular hypertrophy with strain, cardiomyopathy, or may be a normal variant (V1-V3 in young women, aVR). Deep symmetric T wave inversion in anterior leads (Wellens pattern) suggests critical LAD stenosis.
INVT.typical_patient: Wide range; depends on distribution and clinical context.
management_hints: Cardiology referral if new or unexplained
INVT.report_template: T wave inversion noted. Clinical correlation required; ischemia should be excluded in the appropriate clinical context.

IPLMI.summary: Inferoposterolateral myocardial infarction involves combined inferior, posterior, and lateral wall injury, typically from occlusion of a dominant RCA or large LCx artery.
ecg_features: Q waves in II, III, aVF (inferior)
Q waves or ST changes in V5-V6 (lateral)
ST elevation inferolateral in acute phase
Reciprocal ST depression in anterior leads
IPLMI.clinical_significance: Inferoposterolateral MI represents one of the largest MI territories and carries significant risk of LV dysfunction, heart failure, and arrhythmias. Requires urgent reperfusion.
IPLMI.typical_patient: Patients with significant multi-vessel coronary disease or dominant vessel occlusion.
IPLMI.report_template: ECG changes consistent with inferoposterolateral myocardial infarction. Urgent reperfusion evaluation required.

IPMI.summary: Inferoposterior myocardial infarction involves combined inferior and posterior wall injury, typically from RCA occlusion with posterior extension.
ecg_features: Q waves in II, III, aVF
Tall R waves in V1-V2 (posterior changes)
ST depression V1-V3 (reciprocal to posterior elevation)
IPMI.clinical_significance: Posterior extension of inferior MI increases the area at risk and worsens prognosis. Posterior leads (V7-V9) should be obtained to confirm ST elevation in the posterior territory.
IPMI.typical_patient: Patients with RCA disease presenting with acute inferior MI.
management_hints: Monitor for AV block and RV failure
IPMI.report_template: ECG changes consistent with inferoposterior myocardial infarction. Posterior and right-sided leads recommended. Urgent clinical correlation required.

IRBBB.summary: Incomplete right bundle branch block is a partial conduction delay in the right bundle with QRS duration 110-119ms and rSR' pattern in V1.
ecg_features: Wide S waves in I, V5-V6
IRBBB.clinical_significance: Incomplete RBBB is often a normal variant, particularly in young individuals and athletes. It may also be seen with atrial septal defect or early right heart strain. Usually benign without additional findings.
IRBBB.typical_patient: Young adults as normal variant, or patients with right heart abnormalities.
management_hints: Echocardiogram to exclude ASD or structural disease
Monitor for progression to complete RBBB
Cardiology referral if new or associated with symptoms
IRBBB.report_template: Incomplete right bundle branch block. Usually benign; clinical correlation and echocardiogram recommended if clinically indicated.

ISCAL.summary: Ischemic ST-T changes in anterolateral leads, including ST depression and T wave inversion in I, aVL, and V4-V6, indicating anterolateral myocardial ischemia.
ecg_features: ST depression in anterolateral leads (I, aVL, V4-V6)
T wave inversion or flattening anterolateral
ISCAL.clinical_significance: Anterolateral ischemic changes indicate compromised blood supply to the anterior and lateral walls, most commonly from LAD or circumflex territory disease. Requires correlation with symptoms and troponin measurement.
ISCAL.typical_patient: Patients with known or suspected coronary artery disease.
ISCAL.report_template: ST-T changes in anterolateral leads consistent with myocardial ischemia. Clinical correlation and troponin measurement required.

ISCAN.summary: Ischemic changes in anterior leads with ST depression or T wave inversion in V1-V4, suggesting anterior myocardial ischemia.
ecg_features: ST depression or T wave inversion in V1-V4
May have deep symmetric T inversion (Wellens)
ISCAN.clinical_significance: Anterior ischemic changes may represent LAD territory ischemia. Deep symmetric T wave inversion in V1-V4 (Wellens syndrome) indicates critical LAD stenosis requiring urgent intervention.
management_hints: Avoid stress testing in Wellens pattern
ISCAN.report_template: Anterior ischemic ST-T changes noted. Serial troponin and clinical correlation required.

ISCAS.summary: Ischemic changes in anteroseptal leads with ST-T abnormalities in V1-V3, suggesting anteroseptal ischemia.
ecg_features: T wave inversion or flattening anteroseptal
ISCAS.clinical_significance: Anteroseptal ischemia indicates involvement of the proximal LAD or septal perforator territory. Serial monitoring and troponin testing are required.
ISCAS.report_template: Anteroseptal ischemic changes noted. Clinical correlation and troponin measurement recommended.

ISCIL.summary: Ischemic ST-T changes in inferolateral leads, suggesting inferolateral myocardial ischemia from RCA or LCx territory involvement.
ecg_features: ST depression in II, III, aVF, V5-V6
ISCIL.clinical_significance: Inferolateral ischemia suggests multi-vessel disease or dominant vessel involvement. Correlation with symptoms and troponin measurement is essential.
ISCIL.typical_patient: Patients with multi-vessel coronary artery disease.
ISCIL.report_template: Inferolateral ischemic ST-T changes noted. Clinical correlation and troponin measurement required.

ISCIN.summary: Ischemic changes in inferior leads with ST depression or T wave inversion in II, III, and aVF, indicating inferior myocardial ischemia.
ecg_features: ST depression in II, III, aVF
ISCIN.clinical_significance: Inferior ischemic changes indicate RCA or LCx territory ischemia. Must be distinguished from acute inferior STEMI (which shows ST elevation). Serial ECG and troponin are required.
ISCIN.typical_patient: Patients with RCA or LCx disease.
ISCIN.report_template: Inferior ischemic ST-T changes noted. Serial troponin and clinical correlation required.

ISCLA.summary: Ischemic changes in lateral leads with ST depression and T wave inversion in I, aVL, V5-V6, indicating lateral myocardial ischemia from circumflex territory disease.
ecg_features: ST depression in I, aVL, V5-V6
ISCLA.clinical_significance: Lateral ischemia suggests left circumflex or obtuse marginal artery disease. Serial troponin testing and cardiology review are required.
ISCLA.report_template: Lateral ischemic ST-T changes noted. Clinical correlation and troponin measurement required.

ISC_.summary: Non-specific ischemic ST-T changes not localized to a specific territory, suggesting diffuse or non-localizing myocardial ischemia.
ecg_features: Widespread ST depression or T wave changes
ISC_.clinical_significance: Non-specific ischemic changes require clinical correlation. They may reflect multi-vessel disease, subendocardial ischemia, or be related to non-cardiac causes. Troponin measurement is essential.
ISC_.typical_patient: Patients with diffuse coronary artery disease or non-cardiac causes of ST-T changes.
ISC_.report_template: Non-specific ischemic ST-T changes present. Clinical correlation, troponin measurement, and cardiology review recommended.

IVCD.summary: Non-specific intraventricular conduction disturbance with mildly prolonged QRS not meeting criteria for complete bundle branch block.
ecg_features: Non-specific morphology not meeting LBBB or RBBB criteria
IVCD.clinical_significance: Non-specific IVCD may indicate early conduction system disease, myocardial fibrosis, or metabolic disturbance. Less prognostically significant than complete bundle branch block but warrants clinical evaluation.
IVCD.typical_patient: Patients with underlying structural heart disease or conduction system abnormalities.
IVCD.report_template: Non-specific intraventricular conduction disturbance noted. Clinical correlation and echocardiogram recommended.

LAFB.summary: Left anterior fascicular block is characterized by left axis deviation due to delayed activation of the anterosuperior division of the left bundle branch.
ecg_features: Small Q waves in I, aVL
Small R waves in II, III, aVF
Normal QRS duration or mildly prolonged
LAFB.clinical_significance: Isolated LAFB is often benign but may be associated with ischemic or structural heart disease. When combined with RBBB (bifascicular block), risk of complete heart block increases, especially if PR interval is also prolonged (trifascicular block).
LAFB.typical_patient: Older adults with hypertension, ischemic heart disease, or idiopathic conduction disease.
management_hints: Evaluate for underlying structural heart disease
Monitor for progression especially if RBBB present
Cardiology referral for bifascicular block with symptoms
LAFB.report_template: Left anterior fascicular block with left axis deviation. Clinical correlation and echocardiogram recommended.

LAO/LAE.summary: Left atrial overload or enlargement indicated by prolonged or bifid P waves in limb leads (P mitrale) or negative terminal deflection in V1.
ecg_features: Bifid or notched P waves in limb leads (P mitrale)
Negative terminal deflection of P wave in V1 > 1mm deep and 40ms wide
LAO/LAE.clinical_significance: Left atrial enlargement is associated with atrial fibrillation risk, mitral valve disease, left ventricular dysfunction, and hypertension. Echocardiogram should be performed to assess LA size and underlying cause.
LAO/LAE.typical_patient: Patients with mitral valve disease, hypertension, or heart failure.
management_hints: Echocardiogram to assess LA size and mitral valve
LAO/LAE.report_template: P wave morphology consistent with left atrial overload or enlargement. Echocardiogram recommended.

LMI.summary: Lateral myocardial infarction involves ischemic injury to the lateral wall of the left ventricle, typically from circumflex artery or obtuse marginal branch occlusion.
ecg_features: Q waves in I, aVL, V5-V6
May be isolated or part of larger MI
LMI.clinical_significance: Lateral MI may be electrically silent (circumflex territory is less well represented on standard ECG). Normal ECG does not exclude lateral MI. Posterior leads may be helpful.
LMI.report_template: ECG changes consistent with lateral myocardial infarction. Clinical and laboratory correlation required.

LNGQT.summary: Prolonged QT interval with QTc exceeding 450ms in men or 470ms in women, predisposing to potentially fatal ventricular arrhythmias including torsades de pointes.
LNGQT.clinical_significance: Long QT syndrome significantly increases risk of torsades de pointes and sudden cardiac death. Causes include congenital LQTS, electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia), and numerous drugs (antiarrhythmics, antibiotics, antipsychotics). Urgent review of medications and electrolytes is required.
LNGQT.typical_patient: Patients on QT-prolonging medications, those with electrolyte disturbances, or individuals with congenital LQTS (often younger).
management_hints: Genetic testing if congenital LQTS suspected
ICD consideration for high-risk congenital LQTS
LNGQT.report_template: Prolonged QTc interval noted. Electrolyte panel, medication review, and cardiology referral required urgently.

LOWT.summary: Low amplitude T waves, a non-specific finding that may reflect electrolyte disturbance, ischemia, pericardial effusion, or be a normal variant.
ecg_features: T waves < 1mm in limb leads or < 2mm in precordial leads
LOWT.clinical_significance: Low T wave amplitude is non-specific and requires clinical correlation. Hypokalemia, hypothyroidism, and pericardial effusion are common causes. In the ischemic context, it may represent a resolving or early ischemic event.
LOWT.typical_patient: Wide range; depends on clinical context.
management_hints: Thyroid function tests if clinically indicated
LOWT.report_template: Low amplitude T waves noted. Non-specific finding; electrolyte check and clinical correlation recommended.

LPFB.summary: Left posterior fascicular block is characterized by right axis deviation due to delayed activation of the posteroinferior division of the left bundle branch.
ecg_features: Small R waves in I, aVL
Small Q waves in II, III, aVF
Diagnosis of exclusion (rule out RVH, COPD, lateral MI)
LPFB.clinical_significance: LPFB is less common than LAFB and usually indicates significant heart disease. It must be distinguished from right ventricular hypertrophy and lateral MI. Combined with RBBB (bifascicular block) is a significant finding.
LPFB.typical_patient: Patients with structural heart disease; less common than LAFB.
LPFB.report_template: Right axis deviation consistent with possible left posterior fascicular block. Right ventricular hypertrophy and lateral MI must be excluded clinically.

LPR.summary: Prolonged PR interval exceeding 200ms indicating delayed atrioventricular conduction, consistent with first degree AV block.
ecg_features: All P waves conduct to QRS
Normal QRS morphology unless combined with bundle branch block
LPR.clinical_significance: Prolonged PR interval is usually benign but may progress to higher-degree block. It is associated with increased risk of atrial fibrillation in long-term follow-up. Review of AV-nodal blocking medications is essential.
LPR.typical_patient: Older adults, athletes, or patients on AV-nodal blocking medications.
management_hints: Cardiology referral if symptomatic or progressing
LPR.report_template: Prolonged PR interval consistent with first degree AV block. Medication review and clinical correlation recommended.

LVH.summary: Left ventricular hypertrophy diagnosed by voltage criteria with or without associated strain pattern, most commonly from chronic pressure overload due to hypertension or aortic stenosis.
ecg_features: Strain pattern: ST depression and T inversion in lateral leads
LVH.clinical_significance: ECG LVH with strain is associated with increased cardiovascular morbidity and mortality. It indicates chronic pressure overload and warrants aggressive risk factor modification. Echocardiogram provides more accurate assessment of true LV mass.
LVH.typical_patient: Patients with long-standing hypertension, aortic stenosis, or hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
management_hints: Echocardiogram to confirm and quantify LVH
LVH.report_template: Voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy with associated strain pattern. Echocardiogram and blood pressure assessment recommended.

LVOLT.summary: Low QRS voltages in the frontal and horizontal leads, defined as QRS amplitude < 5mm in all limb leads and < 10mm in all precordial leads.
ecg_features: QRS amplitude < 5mm in all limb leads
QRS amplitude < 10mm in all precordial leads
LVOLT.clinical_significance: Low voltage has multiple causes including pericardial effusion (most urgent), cardiac tamponade, obesity, COPD with hyperinflation, hypothyroidism, infiltrative cardiomyopathy (amyloidosis), and anasarca. Pericardial effusion with tamponade physiology is a medical emergency.
LVOLT.typical_patient: Varies by cause; elderly with amyloidosis, patients with known malignancy (effusion), or obese/emphysematous patients.
management_hints: Urgent echocardiogram to exclude pericardial effusion and tamponade
Consider cardiac amyloidosis if appropriate clinical features
LVOLT.report_template: Low QRS voltages noted. Urgent echocardiogram to exclude pericardial effusion recommended.

NDT.summary: Non-diagnostic T wave abnormalities that cannot be ascribed to a specific pathological process, representing a non-specific ECG finding.
ecg_features: T wave changes not meeting criteria for specific diagnosis
May include flattening, low amplitude, or borderline inversion
NDT.clinical_significance: Non-diagnostic T abnormalities are a common, non-specific finding requiring clinical correlation. In symptomatic patients, they may represent early ischemia or be drug/metabolic in origin. In asymptomatic patients, they are often incidental.
management_hints: Cardiology referral if persistent or worsening
NDT.report_template: Non-diagnostic T wave abnormalities noted. Clinical correlation recommended.

NORM.summary: Normal ECG with normal sinus rhythm, normal intervals, normal axis, normal QRS morphology, and normal repolarization. No significant abnormality detected.
NORM.clinical_significance: A normal ECG provides important reassurance but does not exclude all cardiac conditions. Conditions such as intermittent arrhythmias, Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome, and early cardiomyopathy may not always be apparent on a resting ECG.
NORM.typical_patient: Any age; represents the expected finding in a healthy individual without cardiac disease.
management_hints: Consider Holter monitor if intermittent symptoms
NORM.report_template: Normal ECG. No significant abnormality detected.

NST_.summary: Non-specific ST changes that do not meet criteria for a specific diagnosis, representing a non-specific ECG finding requiring clinical correlation.
ecg_features: Not meeting criteria for ischemia or injury
NST_.clinical_significance: Non-specific ST changes are common and may be caused by a wide variety of conditions including hyperventilation, positional changes, medications, electrolyte disturbances, or be a normal variant. In the appropriate clinical context, ischemia must be excluded.
NST_.typical_patient: Wide range; depends on clinical context.
NST_.report_template: Non-specific ST changes noted. Clinical correlation recommended.

NT_.summary: Non-specific T wave changes including flattening or minor inversion that do not meet criteria for a specific diagnosis.
ecg_features: T wave flattening or minor inversion
NT_.clinical_significance: Non-specific T wave changes are very common and usually non-diagnostic. They may be related to electrolyte disturbances, medications, hyperventilation, or be a normal variant. Ischemia should be excluded in symptomatic patients.
NT_.report_template: Non-specific T wave changes noted. Clinical correlation recommended.

PAC.summary: Atrial premature complexes are ectopic beats originating from the atria before the next expected sinus beat, generally benign but may trigger atrial arrhythmias.
ecg_features: Premature P waves with abnormal morphology
P wave may be hidden in preceding T wave
May occur in bigeminy or trigeminy
PAC.clinical_significance: Isolated PACs are generally benign in structurally normal hearts. Frequent PACs may trigger atrial fibrillation or flutter, particularly in susceptible patients. Very frequent PACs (>10,000/day) may rarely cause tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy.
PAC.typical_patient: Common in all ages; usually benign. More concerning if frequent or associated with symptoms.
management_hints: Reassurance if asymptomatic and structurally normal heart
Cardiology referral if symptomatic or frequent
PAC.report_template: Atrial premature complexes noted. Generally benign; clinical correlation and lifestyle modification recommended.

PACE.summary: Normal functioning artificial pacemaker rhythm with appropriate pacing spikes, capture, and sensing, indicating properly functioning cardiac pacing device.
ecg_features: Wide QRS complexes following pacing spikes (ventricular pacing)
LBBB morphology with right ventricular pacing
May have underlying rhythm in demand pacing mode
AV sequential pacing shows both atrial and ventricular spikes
PACE.clinical_significance: Pacemaker ECG interpretation requires knowledge of the pacing mode. Normal pacemaker function must be confirmed. Failure to pace, failure to capture, and failure to sense are important complications to identify.
common_associations: Atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular response
PACE.typical_patient: Patients with symptomatic bradyarrhythmias, high-degree AV block, or sick sinus syndrome.
management_hints: Verify pacing mode and programmed parameters
Assess for pacemaker malfunction (failure to pace, capture, sense)
PACE.report_template: Normal functioning artificial pacemaker rhythm. Device check recommended at scheduled interval.

PMI.summary: Posterior myocardial infarction involving the posterior wall of the left ventricle, manifested as tall R waves and ST depression in V1-V2 (reciprocal changes to posterior ST elevation).
ecg_features: Tall R waves in V1-V2 (R > S in V1)
Posterior leads V7-V9 show ST elevation
PMI.clinical_significance: Posterior MI is frequently missed on standard 12-lead ECG. Posterior leads (V7-V9) are essential for diagnosis. It is often associated with inferior or lateral MI. Urgent reperfusion therapy is required in acute setting.
PMI.typical_patient: Patients with acute coronary syndrome, particularly those with inferior MI.
management_hints: Right-sided leads to exclude RV infarction
PMI.report_template: ECG changes consistent with posterior myocardial infarction. Posterior leads V7-V9 recommended. Urgent clinical correlation required.

PRC(S).summary: Premature complex(es) of unspecified origin, encompassing both atrial and ventricular ectopic beats.
ecg_features: Premature beats with or without compensatory pause
Occur before the next expected sinus beat
PRC(S).clinical_significance: Premature complexes are common and usually benign. Their significance depends on frequency, morphology, and underlying structural heart disease. Frequent PVCs with underlying structural heart disease warrant further evaluation.
PRC(S).typical_patient: Common in all ages; clinical significance varies.
management_hints: Cardiology referral if frequent or symptomatic
PRC(S).report_template: Premature complex(es) noted. Clinical correlation and electrolyte check recommended.

PSVT.summary: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia is a sudden-onset, regular, narrow complex tachycardia typically at 150-250 bpm, most commonly due to AV nodal re-entry (AVNRT) or AV re-entry (AVRT).
ecg_features: Regular narrow complex tachycardia at 150-250 bpm
P waves may be hidden, retrograde, or absent
ST depression may occur due to rate
PSVT.clinical_significance: PSVT is usually not life-threatening in structurally normal hearts but causes significant symptoms. It can be terminated with vagal maneuvers or adenosine. Recurrent PSVT may require catheter ablation for definitive cure.
PSVT.typical_patient: Young to middle-aged adults, often without structural heart disease. WPW-associated AVRT may present at any age.
management_hints: Adenosine 6-12mg IV if vagal maneuvers fail
Beta-blocker or calcium channel blocker for prevention
Electrophysiology study and catheter ablation for recurrent episodes
12-lead ECG during sinus rhythm to assess for pre-excitation
PSVT.report_template: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia present. Vagal maneuvers or adenosine recommended if hemodynamically stable.

PVC.summary: Ventricular premature complex is an ectopic beat originating from the ventricles, characterized by wide bizarre QRS morphology with compensatory pause.
ecg_features: Retrograde P wave or AV dissociation
PVC.clinical_significance: Isolated PVCs are usually benign in structurally normal hearts. Frequent PVCs (>10% of total beats) may cause tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy. PVCs in the setting of structural heart disease or post-MI carry prognostic significance.
PVC.typical_patient: Common in all ages; more significant in those with structural heart disease.
management_hints: Cardiology referral if frequent, symptomatic, or associated with structural disease
Beta-blocker or antiarrhythmic if treatment required
PVC.report_template: Ventricular premature complex(es) noted. Electrolyte check and clinical correlation recommended.

QWAVE.summary: Pathological Q waves indicating prior myocardial necrosis, defined as duration > 40ms or amplitude > 25% of the R wave in two contiguous leads.
ecg_features: Q wave amplitude > 25% of R wave
Present in two or more contiguous leads
May be associated with T wave inversion
QWAVE.clinical_significance: Pathological Q waves indicate prior transmural myocardial infarction. Their territory guides localization of the infarcted area. New Q waves suggest recent MI. Septal Q waves in lateral leads are normal and should not be mistaken for infarct Q waves.
QWAVE.typical_patient: Patients with prior history of myocardial infarction.
QWAVE.report_template: Pathological Q waves noted consistent with prior myocardial infarction. Clinical correlation and echocardiogram recommended.

RAO/RAE.summary: Right atrial overload or enlargement indicated by peaked P waves (P pulmonale) in inferior leads, reflecting increased right atrial pressure or volume.
ecg_features: Peaked P waves > 2.5mm in II, III, aVF (P pulmonale)
RAO/RAE.clinical_significance: Right atrial enlargement suggests chronic pulmonary disease, pulmonary hypertension, tricuspid valve disease, or congenital heart disease. Echocardiogram is essential to assess right heart size and function.
RAO/RAE.typical_patient: Patients with chronic lung disease, pulmonary hypertension, or right-sided valvular heart disease.
management_hints: Right heart catheterization if pulmonary hypertension suspected
RAO/RAE.report_template: P wave morphology consistent with right atrial overload or enlargement. Echocardiogram and pulmonary evaluation recommended.

RVH.summary: Right ventricular hypertrophy characterized by right axis deviation, tall R waves in V1, and deep S waves in lateral leads, resulting from chronic right ventricular pressure overload.
ecg_features: ST depression and T inversion in V1-V3 (right ventricular strain)
RVH.clinical_significance: RVH indicates significant right heart pressure overload, most commonly from pulmonary hypertension or chronic lung disease. It is associated with increased morbidity and mortality. Echocardiogram and cardiopulmonary evaluation are essential.
RVH.typical_patient: Patients with chronic pulmonary disease, pulmonary hypertension, or congenital heart disease.
management_hints: CT pulmonary angiography to exclude PE
RVH.report_template: ECG features consistent with right ventricular hypertrophy. Echocardiogram and pulmonary evaluation recommended.

SARRH.summary: Sinus arrhythmia is a normal physiological variation in heart rate associated with respiration, accelerating with inspiration and decelerating with expiration.
ecg_features: Irregular RR intervals varying with respiration
Normal P wave morphology and axis
Rate variation > 10% between fastest and slowest rates
SARRH.clinical_significance: Sinus arrhythmia is a normal physiological finding, particularly prominent in young individuals and athletes with high vagal tone. It requires no treatment and carries an excellent prognosis.
SARRH.typical_patient: Young adults and athletes; may diminish with age.
management_hints: Differentiate from pathological arrhythmias if uncertain
SARRH.report_template: Sinus arrhythmia present. Normal physiological variant. No action required.

SBRAD.summary: Sinus bradycardia with heart rate less than 60 bpm arising from the sinoatrial node, which may be physiological or pathological depending on the clinical context.
ecg_features: Regular sinus rhythm with rate < 60 bpm
Normal P wave morphology and axis
SBRAD.clinical_significance: Sinus bradycardia is physiological in athletes and during sleep. Pathological causes include sick sinus syndrome, inferior MI, hypothyroidism, increased intracranial pressure, and medication effects (beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin). Symptomatic bradycardia requires urgent evaluation.
SBRAD.typical_patient: Athletes, well-trained individuals, or patients on negative chronotropic medications. Pathological in elderly with sick sinus syndrome.
management_hints: Pacemaker if symptomatic sick sinus syndrome
SBRAD.report_template: Sinus bradycardia present. Clinical correlation recommended; investigate for underlying cause if symptomatic.

SEHYP.summary: Septal hypertrophy suggested by deep but narrow Q waves in lateral leads, which may indicate hypertrophic cardiomyopathy or represent a normal septal activation pattern.
ecg_features: Deep narrow Q waves in I, aVL, V4-V6
Q wave amplitude may be large
May have left ventricular hypertrophy criteria
SEHYP.clinical_significance: Septal Q waves in lateral leads can indicate septal hypertrophy, particularly in the context of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM). They must be distinguished from pathological Q waves of infarction (broader). Echocardiogram is essential for diagnosis.
SEHYP.typical_patient: Young adults with HCM or older patients with hypertensive septal hypertrophy.
management_hints: Echocardiogram to assess septal thickness and LV outflow
Avoid intense competitive sports until HCM excluded
SEHYP.report_template: Deep narrow Q waves in lateral leads suggesting possible septal hypertrophy. Echocardiogram recommended to exclude hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

SR.summary: Normal sinus rhythm with heart rate 60-100 bpm, regular RR intervals, and P waves originating from the sinoatrial node preceding each QRS complex.
ecg_features: Positive P waves in I, II, aVF
SR.clinical_significance: Sinus rhythm confirms normal sinoatrial node function and atrioventricular conduction. It is the expected normal rhythm and provides a baseline against which other abnormalities are assessed.
SR.typical_patient: Normal finding in any healthy individual.
management_hints: No specific action required for sinus rhythm itself
Assess other ECG parameters for abnormalities
SR.report_template: Normal sinus rhythm. Rate and rhythm are regular.

STACH.summary: Sinus tachycardia with heart rate exceeding 100 bpm, arising from the sinoatrial node as a physiological response to increased sympathetic tone or pathological stressor.
ecg_features: Regular sinus rhythm with rate > 100 bpm
Normal P wave morphology and axis
Normal PR interval (may shorten at high rates)
May have minor non-specific ST-T changes
STACH.clinical_significance: Sinus tachycardia is always secondary to an underlying cause and rarely requires treatment of the rhythm itself. Common causes include fever, pain, hypovolemia, anemia, pulmonary embolism, thyrotoxicosis, and heart failure. Identifying and treating the underlying cause is paramount.
STACH.typical_patient: Any patient under physiological or pathological stress. Rate typically proportional to the degree of stress.
management_hints: Do NOT treat the tachycardia itself without addressing the cause
Pulmonary embolism must be excluded if rate is persistent
STACH.report_template: Sinus tachycardia present. Investigation for underlying cause required.

STD_.summary: Non-specific ST depression not meeting criteria for a specific diagnosis, requiring clinical correlation to determine significance.
STD_.clinical_significance: Non-specific ST depression is a common ECG finding with a broad differential. In symptomatic patients it may represent ischemia. Other causes include LVH with strain, digitalis effect, hypokalemia, and tachycardia. Clinical context is essential for interpretation.
STD_.typical_patient: Wide range; depends on clinical context.
STD_.report_template: Non-specific ST depression noted. Clinical correlation and electrolyte check recommended.

STE_.summary: Non-specific ST elevation not localizing to a STEMI territory, which may represent early repolarization, pericarditis, or other non-ischemic causes.
ecg_features: May be present in multiple leads
STE_.clinical_significance: Non-specific ST elevation must be carefully distinguished from acute STEMI. Early repolarization variant is common in young males. Pericarditis shows diffuse ST elevation with PR depression. Any new ST elevation with chest pain should be urgently evaluated.
STE_.typical_patient: Young males for early repolarization; any age for pericarditis.
management_hints: Urgent clinical correlation to exclude STEMI
Consider pericarditis if diffuse with PR depression
STE_.report_template: Non-specific ST elevation noted. Urgent clinical correlation to exclude acute MI required.

SVARR.summary: Supraventricular arrhythmia of unspecified type originating above the Bundle of His, encompassing atrial and junctional arrhythmias.
ecg_features: Narrow QRS complex (< 120ms) unless aberrantly conducted
Abnormal P wave morphology or absent P waves
SVARR.clinical_significance: Supraventricular arrhythmia encompasses multiple rhythm disorders including atrial tachycardia, AVNRT, AVRT, and junctional tachycardia. Further characterization of the specific arrhythmia type guides management.
SVARR.typical_patient: Wide range depending on specific arrhythmia type.
management_hints: Rate control with AV-nodal blocking agents
SVARR.report_template: Supraventricular arrhythmia present. Cardiology review for further characterization and management recommended.

SVTAC.summary: Supraventricular tachycardia, a rapid regular arrhythmia originating above the Bundle of His, typically presenting as a narrow complex tachycardia at 150-250 bpm.
ecg_features: P waves may be absent, retrograde, or hidden
ST depression may occur due to rate
SVTAC.clinical_significance: SVT causes significant symptoms and may cause hemodynamic compromise at very high rates. It is generally not life-threatening in structurally normal hearts. Adenosine terminates most SVTs and helps in diagnosis.
SVTAC.typical_patient: Young to middle-aged adults, often without structural heart disease.
management_hints: Adenosine 6-12mg IV if vagal maneuvers fail
SVTAC.report_template: Supraventricular tachycardia present. Vagal maneuvers or adenosine recommended if stable.

TAB_.summary: T wave abnormality encompassing a variety of T wave changes including inversion, flattening, biphasic pattern, or peaked T waves that deviate from normal.
ecg_features: May be inverted, flat, biphasic, or peaked
Associated ST changes may be present
TAB_.clinical_significance: T wave abnormalities are common non-specific findings. Peaked T waves may suggest hyperkalemia or early ischemia (hyperacute T waves). Inverted T waves may indicate ischemia, strain, or normal variant. Clinical context is essential.
TAB_.typical_patient: Wide range; depends on type and distribution of T wave abnormality.
TAB_.report_template: T wave abnormality noted. Clinical correlation and electrolyte check recommended.

TRIGU.summary: Trigeminal pattern with every third beat being a premature complex (atrial or ventricular), creating a repeating triplet pattern.
ecg_features: Every third beat is a premature complex
Premature beats may be atrial or ventricular
Effective heart rate may be reduced
TRIGU.clinical_significance: Trigeminy may be benign in structurally normal hearts but can indicate underlying ischemia, electrolyte disturbance, or drug toxicity. Sustained trigeminy with underlying structural disease warrants investigation.
TRIGU.typical_patient: Can occur at any age; significance depends on underlying structural disease.
TRIGU.report_template: Trigeminal pattern noted. Electrolyte check and clinical correlation recommended.

VCLVH.summary: Voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy based on QRS amplitude measurements, most commonly the Sokolow-Lyon or Cornell criteria, without necessarily showing the strain pattern.
ecg_features: High precordial or limb lead voltages
VCLVH.clinical_significance: Voltage criteria alone have moderate sensitivity and specificity for true anatomic LVH. They are common in young thin individuals as false positives. Echocardiogram is required for confirmation and quantification of LV mass.
VCLVH.typical_patient: Hypertensive patients or young thin adults as normal variant.
management_hints: Cardiology referral if confirmed LVH with symptoms
VCLVH.report_template: Voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy met. Echocardiogram recommended for confirmation.

WPW.summary: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome is a pre-excitation syndrome caused by an accessory pathway (Bundle of Kent) bypassing the AV node, resulting in short PR interval and delta waves.
ecg_features: Positive delta in left-sided pathway; negative in right-sided
WPW.clinical_significance: WPW is associated with risk of paroxysmal SVT and, critically, potentially fatal rapid pre-excited atrial fibrillation if the accessory pathway has a short refractory period. AV-nodal blocking agents (adenosine, digoxin, verapamil) are contraindicated in pre-excited AF as they may accelerate conduction over the accessory pathway.
WPW.typical_patient: Young adults, often identified incidentally or following symptomatic arrhythmia. May present at any age.
management_hints: Avoid AV-nodal blocking agents if pre-excited AF
Catheter ablation of accessory pathway (highly effective)
Avoid competitive sports until risk assessment complete
Family screening not routinely indicated unless familial WPW
WPW.report_template: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome with short PR interval and delta waves. Electrophysiology referral recommended. Avoid AV-nodal blocking agents.
